title: Rotated Sorted Array with Duplicate Elements and Edge Cases

statement.markdown: Given a sorted array that may contain duplicates and possibly large indices (1 to 10000), find the first index of the target element. If not found, return -1. The array will be rotated so the smallest number will be to the right of the largest number.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a list of integers in the format [n, arr] where n is the size of the input list and arr is the list itself.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be the first index of the target element, or -1 if not found.
statement.notes: The array will be sorted and rotated, and may contain duplicates. The indices will be 1 to n where n is the size of the array.

examples.explanation: The target element 4 is at index 1.
The target element 3 is not present in the array.

hints.text: Consider using a modified binary search algorithm that handles duplicate elements.

editorial.title: Editorial for Rotated Sorted Array with Duplicate Elements and Edge Cases
editorial.intuition: The key insight is to find the first index of the target element even when the array contains duplicates and possibly large indices.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve sorting the array, which is not efficient for large arrays.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a modified binary search algorithm that handles duplicate elements by finding the first index of the target element in the two halves of the array.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The algorithm correctly finds the first index of the target element even when the array contains duplicates and possibly large indices.
editorial.dryRun: Example run of the algorithm on the input [10, [4,5,5,6,7,7,8,8,8,9]]
